provenance.source_corpus: Legacy Arsenic Pollution of Lakes Near Cobalt, Ontario, Canada: Arsenic in Lake Water and Sediment Remains Elevated Nearly a Century After Mining Activity Has Ceased

source_spans.text: Although it is not disputed that historical mining activity has increased arsenic concentrations in certain lakes around Cobalt
Although it is not disputed that historical mining activity has increased arsenic concentrations in certain lakes around Cobalt
These legacy mine tailings remain on land, in rivers, and in lakes and are freely migrating throughout the environment today
This lack of containment has led to the contamination of several lakes in the area that had no historical mining activity in their catchment

nodes.name: Fe-Mn oxides at the water-sediment interface

source_spans.text: Fe-Mn oxides at the water-sediment interface perpetually scavenge arsenic from buried tailings below and from contaminated surface waters that cause arsenic concentrations to remain enriched in the upper sediments

nodes.name: arsenic concentrations in the upper sediments

source_spans.text: Fe-Mn oxides at the water-sediment interface perpetually scavenge arsenic from buried tailings below and from contaminated surface waters that cause arsenic concentrations to remain enriched in the upper sediments
There is a strong relationship between the arsenic concentration in surface water and surface sediments in the study lakes
There is a strong relationship between the arsenic concentration in surface water and surface sediments in the study lakes
Even though background concentrations are naturally elevated, the presence of arsenic-rich tailings in these lakes has prevented any natural recovery from occurring.
Even though background concentrations are naturally elevated, the presence of arsenic-rich tailings in these lakes has prevented any natural recovery from occurring.
